background: Refer to the first-order model for auction price $(y)$ considered in Example 12.1.

task.query: Interpret the estimates of the $ eta$ parameters in the model.
task.answer: The least squares prediction equation, as given in Example 12.1, is $	ext{hat}{y}=-1,339+ 12.74 x_1+85.95 x_2$. We know that with first-order models, $ eta_1$ represents the slope of the line relating $y$ to $x_1$ for fixed $x_2$. That is, $ eta_1$ measures the change in $E(y)$ for every 1-unit increase in $x_1$ when the other independent variable in the model is held fixed. A similar statement can be made about $ eta_2:  eta_2$ measures the change in $E(y)$ for every 1-unit increase in $x_2$ when the other $x$ in the model is held fixed.

task.query: Provide the interpretation of $	ext{hat}{ eta}_1$.
task.answer: $	ext{hat}{ eta}_1=12.74$ : We estimate the mean auction price $E(y)$ of an antique clock to increase $$ 12.74$ for every 1-year increase in age ( $x_1$ ) when the number of bidders ( $x_2$ ) is held fixed.

task.query: Provide the interpretation of $	ext{hat}{ eta}_2$.
task.answer: $	ext{hat}{ eta}_2=85.95$ : We estimate the mean auction price $E(y)$ of an antique clock to increase $$ 85.95$ for every 1-bidder increase in the number of bidders ( $x_2$ ) when age $	ext{left(x_1ight}$ is held fixed.

task.query: Explain why $	ext{hat}{ eta}_0$ does not have a meaningful interpretation in this example.
task.answer: The value $	ext{hat}{ eta}_0=-1,339$ does not have a meaningful interpretation in this example. To see this, note that $	ext{hat}{y}=	ext{hat}{ eta}_0$ when $x_1=x_2=0$. Thus, $	ext{hat}{ eta}_0=-1,339$ represents the estimated mean auction price when the values of all the independent variables are set equal to 0. Because an antique clock with these characteristics - an age of 0 years and 0 bidders on the clock-is not practical, the value of $	ext{hat}{ eta}_0$ has no meaningful interpretation.

meta_info.caption: Interpreting the $\beta$ Estimates: Clock Auction Price Model
